source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 15.3.5 chattr (pp. 297-299)

questions.q: What is the purpose of chattr?
options: To set file/directory attributes that protect important files from accidental deletion or modification
questions.why: chattr sets low-level attributes (like i and a) that guard files even against root until removed.

questions.q: Make the file /etc/resolv.conf immutable (cannot be modified or deleted).
questions.why: +i makes a file immutable - not even root can change or delete it until you run chattr -i.

questions.q: With the 'i' (immutable) attribute set on a FILE, what can you still do?
options: Only view it - you cannot delete, modify, or append to it
Append to it but not delete it
Delete it but not modify it
questions.why: Immutable blocks delete, modify and append; only viewing remains.

questions.q: Set the append-only attribute on the log file /etc/tmpfile1.
questions.why: +a allows appending to a file (e.g. >>) but forbids deleting or otherwise modifying it - useful for logs.

questions.q: View the attributes of the file /tmp/filei with lsattr.
questions.why: lsattr lists a file's chattr attributes (e.g. ----i---------e-----).

questions.q: Remove the immutable attribute from the file /tmp/filei.
questions.why: chattr -i clears the immutable attribute so the file can be changed again.
